topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My breath hitched in my throat, the air suddenly too thick to draw in. The subject line was innocuous, but the body of the message contained a carefully worded confession: my piece, the one I’d poured months into, was credited to a completely different author. A cold sweat prickled my palms, making the keyboard feel slick and alien beneath my fingers. I reread the text, my eyes blurring slightly, searching for a mistake, a typo that could explain the colossal error. There was nothing. Just the stark, undeniable truth. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared blankly at the wall. A slow, shaky exhale escaped me, followed by a sudden, desperate need to pace the small office, the rhythm of my footsteps uneven and frantic.

[story 2]

I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide, startled eyes. The notification popped up, and the world seemed to tilt. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, a nauseating sensation spreading through my chest. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure of my effort, a theft of my voice. I picked up the printout of the published article, my hands trembling so badly the paper rattled. Each line felt like an accusation. I dropped the paper onto the desk with a sharp slap, the sound echoing in the quiet room. I pushed my chair back violently, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding deafening. I needed air, desperately. I stumbled toward the window, needing the vast, indifferent sky to swallow the churning confusion inside me.

[story 3]

When the notification finally registered, a strange stillness descended, a vacuum where my usual internal chatter used to be. My hands, resting on the cool surface of the wooden table, began to shake almost imperceptibly at first, then with increasing force. I blinked several times, trying to force the information into a logical framework, but the pieces refused to connect. The reality of the situation—that someone else was claiming my work—felt too large, too heavy to bear. I stood up abruptly, my legs feeling unsteady, as if gravity had suddenly increased its pull. I walked to the door, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, my gaze fixed on the doorknob, a silent plea for an escape route from the suffocating pressure building behind my ribs.
